ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pork floss; mapped to broader pork food product class. A more specific term for dried shredded pork is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat and meat products — dried/dehydrated processed pork product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for pork floss (rou song). Code A0CKW for processed/dried meat products used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork floss as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crispy pork floss is approximately 10 g (about 1 tablespoon), commonly used as a topping or garnish over congee, bread, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Floss — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for crispy pork floss (cr_id F06110004) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Protein-Carbohydrate Foods — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for crispy pork floss (rou song) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate fraction (52.2 g/100g, predominantly maltose and sugar at 47.6 g/100g). Maltose has a published GI of approximately 105 (University of Sydney GI Database); however, the high protein content (32.3 g/100g) and dietary fibre (3.0 g/100g) substantially moderate the effective glycaemic response, yielding a blended analogous estimate of GI 55. Per standard GI classification (University of Sydney), GI 55 falls in the low category (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per 10 g serving = (55 × 5.22) / 100 = 2.87, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1367 mg per 100 g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid crispy pork floss or restrict use to a trace garnish of 1–2 g; even a 10 g serving delivers 137 mg sodium, contributing significantly to the 1500–2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content at 47.6 g per 100 g (predominantly maltose and added sugars) with total carbohydrate at 52.2 g/100 g, posing a severe glycaemic risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid crispy pork floss as a regular topping; the sugar content of 47.6 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, and even small servings can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of very high sodium (1367 mg/100 g) and high added sugar (47.6 g/100 g) elevates cardiovascular risk through blood pressure and metabolic pathways; pork-derived saturated fat and arachidonic acid further contribute
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate crispy pork floss from their diet due to its extreme sodium load and high sugar content, both of which are independently associated with increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1367 mg/100 g) worsens tissue oedema; high sugar (47.6 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; pork contains arachidonic acid amplifying inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid crispy pork floss entirely, as its combination of high sodium, high sugar, and arachidonic acid from pork collectively amplifies systemic inflammation and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy pork floss (Chinese: 猪肉松 / 豬肉鬆, Mandarin: rou song or zhu rou song) is a dried, shredded pork product widely consumed across Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore, China, Taiwan, and Southeast Asia. It is produced by slow-braising pork in a seasoned broth containing soy sauce, sugar, maltose, salt, and sesame oil, then shredding the meat into fine fibres and stir-frying or dehydrating it until it achieves a light, fluffy, and crispy texture. The resulting product is golden-brown, intensely savoury-sweet, and has a melt-in-the-mouth quality. Crispy pork floss is commonly used as a topping or garnish over congee (rice porridge), steamed white rice, bread rolls, sushi rolls, and savoury pastries. It is also eaten as a standalone snack. Nutritionally, crispy pork floss is a high-protein food (32.3 g/100 g) but is also very high in sugar (47.6 g/100 g, predominantly from added maltose and honey) and sodium (1367 mg/100 g), making it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The carbohydrate content is 52.2 g/100 g with only 3.0 g/100 g dietary fibre, indicating a highly refined carbohydrate profile. Energy density is 392 kcal/100 g. Despite its protein richness, the extreme sodium and sugar burden means it should be used only as a trace garnish (1–2 g) in clinical dietary contexts. It is not halal-certified as it contains pork, and contains soy and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: Is pork floss high in sodium?
How many calories in pork floss?
Is pork floss suitable for hypertension?
Can I eat pork floss if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06110004) with a confidence of 0.95. This is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but all nutrient values are exact matches from the approved source.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for crispy pork floss are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F06110004) as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not have a direct entry for this specific product formulation. 2. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for crispy pork floss exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the dominant maltose/sugar carbohydrate fraction moderated by high protein content. Per University of Sydney GI classification, GI 55 is classified as low (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' accordingly. 3. Pork floss is not halal as it is derived from pork. 4. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.353 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database per Project Veritas source policy. 5. FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as the closest analogous anchor for dried/processed pork products; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for pork floss exists. 6. All core macros (protein_g 32.3, fat_g 5.9, carbohydrate_g 52.2) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
